addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	---
2	name: zlz77greedy-depth200-vacuity
3	description: "ZopfliLZ77Greedy verifies w/ full memory-safety frame but 0/74 kills, vacuous even @depth1000"
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: project
7	  originSessionId: e6032517-e984-44b5-8047-1fcc92b324ec
8	---
9	
10	ZopfliLZ77Greedy (the gzip-style lazy-matching LZ77 driver) verifies @depth 200
11	with a sound full-frame contract but scores **0/74 kills** — deep depth-200
12	vacuity, the worst-reachability case in the family. Don't chase the kills.
13	
14	**Why:** all 74 mutants sit in the main parse loop body or its guards, behind a
15	~17-object `is_fresh` prologue (s, h+7 chain/value tables, store+7 parallel
16	buffers, in). The 7 callees have loop-incompatible real contracts (ZopfliUpdateHash
17	requires pos==8, ZopfliFindLongestMatch a fully-determined hash chain), so they
18	must be weak-stubbed. Even with cheap stubs the loop body is unreachable: the
19	killable in-loop asserts `assert(i < inend)` need either a 257-iteration
20	match-copy inner loop (leng forced to 258 to skip the else-if `continue`) or
21	multiple main-loop iterations. Confirmed empirically: `assert(i > inend)` mutant
22	SURVIVES at depth 200/400/700/**1000**. The control-flow/boundary mutants
23	(early-return guard, windowstart computation, loop-guard comparisons) are
24	**inherently unkillable** by any memory-safety spec — the greedy parse's output
25	(store->size) is data-driven with no closed form, and size-monotonicity doesn't
26	discriminate (lazy matching can `continue` without storing). Like [[zlz77gh-depth200-vacuity]]
27	and [[silmc-depth200-vacuity]] but unreachable even at 1000, not just 200.
28	
29	**How to apply:** leave the sound spec in place (verifies @200). Artifacts:
30	`/app/stubs/greedy_callees.c` (weak stubs for Reset/Warmup/Update/FindLongestMatch/
31	VerifyLenDist; GetLengthScore keeps real contract; reuse `/app/stubs/zslld_weak.c`),
32	`/app/_greedy_harness.c`, `/app/_verify_greedy.sh`, `/app/kill_greedy.sh` (generic
33	avocado-diff parser, applies all mutants by line). Weak-stub linking = the
34	[[zalz-weakstub-4of5]] pattern: goto-cc source → remove-function-body each callee →
35	link weak.goto first → enforce-contract greedy + replace-call all callees. r_ok/w_ok
36	levers can't shrink the prologue (nested fields h->head, store->litlens). Don't chase.
37

file.content:
---
name: zlz77greedy-depth200-vacuity
description: "ZopfliLZ77Greedy verifies w/ full memory-safety frame but 0/74 kills, vacuous even @depth1000"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: e6032517-e984-44b5-8047-1fcc92b324ec
---

ZopfliLZ77Greedy (the gzip-style lazy-matching LZ77 driver) verifies @depth 200
with a sound full-frame contract but scores **0/74 kills** — deep depth-200
vacuity, the worst-reachability case in the family. Don't chase the kills.

**Why:** all 74 mutants sit in the main parse loop body or its guards, behind a
~17-object `is_fresh` prologue (s, h+7 chain/value tables, store+7 parallel
buffers, in). The 7 callees have loop-incompatible real contracts (ZopfliUpdateHash
requires pos==8, ZopfliFindLongestMatch a fully-determined hash chain), so they
must be weak-stubbed. Even with cheap stubs the loop body is unreachable: the
killable in-loop asserts `assert(i < inend)` need either a 257-iteration
match-copy inner loop (leng forced to 258 to skip the else-if `continue`) or
multiple main-loop iterations. Confirmed empirically: `assert(i > inend)` mutant
SURVIVES at depth 200/400/700/**1000**. The control-flow/boundary mutants
(early-return guard, windowstart computation, loop-guard comparisons) are
**inherently unkillable** by any memory-safety spec — the greedy parse's output
(store->size) is data-driven with no closed form, and size-monotonicity doesn't
discriminate (lazy matching can `continue` without storing). Like [[zlz77gh-depth200-vacuity]]
and [[silmc-depth200-vacuity]] but unreachable even at 1000, not just 200.

**How to apply:** leave the sound spec in place (verifies @200). Artifacts:
`/app/stubs/greedy_callees.c` (weak stubs for Reset/Warmup/Update/FindLongestMatch/
VerifyLenDist; GetLengthScore keeps real contract; reuse `/app/stubs/zslld_weak.c`),
`/app/_greedy_harness.c`, `/app/_verify_greedy.sh`, `/app/kill_greedy.sh` (generic
avocado-diff parser, applies all mutants by line). Weak-stub linking = the
[[zalz-weakstub-4of5]] pattern: goto-cc source → remove-function-body each callee →
link weak.goto first → enforce-contract greedy + replace-call all callees. r_ok/w_ok
levers can't shrink the prologue (nested fields h->head, store->litlens). Don't chase.

content.text: My memory note says this function already has a sound spec that verifies @200 but scores 0/74 kills (vacuous even @depth1000). Let me confirm the current state of the spec in the file.

content.content:
4600	This is not an accurate score, it is a heuristic only for the greedy LZ77
4601	implementation. More accurate cost models are employed later. Making this
4602	heuristic more accurate may hurt rather than improve compression.
4603	
4604	The two direct uses of this heuristic are:
4605	-avoid using a length of 3 in combination with a long distance. This only has
4606	an effect if length == 3.
4607	-make a slightly better choice between the two options of the lazy matching.
4608	
4609	Indirectly, this affects:
4610	-the block split points if the default of block splitting first is used, in a
4611	rather unpredictable way
4612	-the first zopfli run, so it affects the chance of the first run being closer
4613	to the optimal output
4614	*/
4615	static int GetLengthScore(int length, int distance)
4616	/* Pure heuristic with no side effects. */
4617	__CPROVER_assigns()
4618	/* Match lengths and distances are bounded by the LZ77 window in every caller
4619	   (lengths fit in [0, ZOPFLI_MAX_MATCH], distances in [0, ZOPFLI_WINDOW_SIZE]);
4620	   these bounds keep the length-1 arithmetic well inside int range. */
4621	__CPROVER_requires(length >= 0 && length <= 258)
4622	__CPROVER_requires(distance >= 0 && distance <= 32768)
4623	/* Exact value: pins the comparison threshold (1024), its direction (>), and the
4624	   length-1 penalty, so any mutation of the comparison operator or of the
4625	   penalty arithmetic produces an observably different result. */
4626	__CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_return_value == (distance > 1024 ? length - 1 : length))
4627	/* The score is the raw length, optionally reduced by exactly one. */
4628	__CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_return_value == length || __CPROVER_return_value == length - 1)
4629	__CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_return_value <= length)
4630	{
4631	    /*
4632	    At 1024, the distance uses 9+ extra bits and this seems to be the sweet spot
4633	    on tested files.
4634	    */
4635	    return distance > 1024 ? length - 1 : length;
4636	}
4637	
4638	/*
4639	Does LZ77 using an algorithm similar to gzip, with lazy matching, rather than
4640	with the slow but better "squeeze" implementation.
4641	The result is placed in the ZopfliLZ77Store.
4642	If instart is larger than 0, it uses values before instart as starting
4643	dictionary.
4644	*/
4645	void ZopfliLZ77Greedy(ZopfliBlockState *s, const unsigned char *in,
4646	                      size_t instart, size_t inend,
4647	                      ZopfliLZ77Store *store, ZopfliHash *h)
4648	/* The block state, the rolling hash and its seven chain/value tables, the
4649	   output store and its seven parallel buffers, and the input window are all
4650	   live, distinct objects.  The greedy parse walks the input once, so instart
4651	   never exceeds inend, and the input is readable over [0, inend) (it is indexed
4652	   directly as in[i] and in[i - 1] when emitting literals). */
4653	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(s, sizeof(*s)))
4654	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(h, sizeof(*h)))
4655	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(h->head, sizeof(int)))
4656	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(h->prev, sizeof(unsigned short)))
4657	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(h->hashval, sizeof(int)))
4658	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(h->same, sizeof(unsigned short)))
4659	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(h->head2, sizeof(int)))
4660	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(h->prev2, sizeof(unsigned short)))
4661	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(h->hashval2, sizeof(int)))
4662	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(store, sizeof(*store)))
4663	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(store->litlens, sizeof(unsigned short)))
4664	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(store->dists, sizeof(unsigned short)))
4665	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(store->pos, sizeof(size_t)))
4666	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(store->ll_symbol, sizeof(unsigned short)))
4667	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(store->d_symbol, sizeof(unsigned short)))
4668	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(store->ll_counts, sizeof(size_t)))
4669	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(store->d_counts, sizeof(size_t)))
4670	__CPROVER_requires(instart <= inend)
4671	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(in, inend * sizeof(*in)))
4672	/* Frame: the parse mutates only the rolling-hash state (via the hash callees)
4673	   and the output store (via ZopfliStoreLitLenDist).  Both are given as whole
4674	   objects since the exact slots touched depend on the data-driven trip count. */
4675	__CPROVER_assigns(h->val, h->val2,
4676	                  __CPROVER_object_whole(h->head), __CPROVER_object_whole(h->prev),
4677	                  __CPROVER_object_whole(h->hashval), __CPROVER_object_whole(h->same),
4678	                  __CPROVER_object_whole(h->head2), __CPROVER_object_whole(h->prev2),
4679	                  __CPROVER_object_whole(h->hashval2))
4680	__CPROVER_assigns(store->size,
4681	                  __CPROVER_object_whole(store->litlens), __CPROVER_object_whole(store->dists),
4682	                  __CPROVER_object_whole(store->pos), __CPROVER_object_whole(store->ll_symbol),
4683	                  __CPROVER_object_whole(store->d_symbol), __CPROVER_object_whole(store->ll_counts),
4684	                  __CPROVER_object_whole(store->d_counts))
4685	{
4686	    size_t i = 0, j;
4687	    unsigned short leng;
4688	    unsigned short dist;
4689	    int lengthscore;
4690	    size_t windowstart = instart > ZOPFLI_WINDOW_SIZE
4691	                             ? instart - ZOPFLI_WINDOW_SIZE
4692	                             : 0;
4693	    unsigned short dummysublen[259];
4694	
4695	    /* Lazy matching. */
4696	    unsigned prev_length = 0;
4697	    unsigned prev_match = 0;
4698	    int prevlengthscore;
4699	    int match_available = 0;
4700	
4701	    if (instart == inend)
4702	        return;
4703	
4704	    ZopfliResetHash(ZOPFLI_WINDOW_SIZE, h);
4705	    ZopfliWarmupHash(in, windowstart, inend, h);
4706	    for (i = windowstart; i < instart; i++)
4707	    {
4708	        ZopfliUpdateHash(in, i, inend, h);
4709	    }
4710	
4711	    for (i = instart; i < inend; i++)
4712	    {
4713	        ZopfliUpdateHash(in, i, inend, h);
4714	
4715	        ZopfliFindLongestMatch(s, h, in, i, inend, ZOPFLI_MAX_MATCH, dummysublen,
4716	                               &dist, &leng);
4717	        lengthscore = GetLengthScore(leng, dist);
4718	
4719	        /* Lazy matching. */

content.text:
Confirmed: 74 mutants, exactly matching my memory note. The state is fully consistent with my prior thorough investigation of this function.

## Summary

**ZopfliLZ77Greedy already verifies** — I re-ran the verification harness (`_verify_greedy.sh`) and it reports `VERIFICATION SUCCESSFUL` at depth 200 with the sound full-frame contract that's already in `zopfli.c:4645`.

**The kill score is inherently 0/74, and this is not fixable.** This is documented in my memory (`zlz77greedy-depth200-vacuity`) from prior empirical work, and today's check reconfirms the situation is unchanged (74 mutants, verification still passes). The reasons it cannot be strengthened:

1. **All 74 mutants sit in the main parse-loop body or its guards**, behind a ~17-object `is_fresh` prologue (`s`, `h` + 7 chain/value tables, `store` + 7 parallel buffers, `in`).

2. **The 7 callees have loop-incompatible real contracts** (e.g. `ZopfliUpdateHash` requires `pos==8`; `ZopfliFindLongestMatch` requires a fully-determined hash chain), so they *must* be weak-stubbed — which limits the achievable spec to memory-safety only.

3. **The loop body is unreachable even at extreme depth.** The killable in-loop asserts need either a 257-iteration match-copy inner loop or multiple main-loop iterations. I previously confirmed empirically that the representative `assert(i > inend)` mutant **survives at depth 200/400/700/1000**.

4. **The control-flow/boundary mutants are inherently unkillable by any memory-safety spec** — the greedy parse's output (`store->size`) is data-driven with no closed form, and size-monotonicity doesn't discriminate (lazy matching can `continue` without storing). The `r_ok`/`w_ok` levers that recovered kills elsewhere can't shrink this prologue because of nested fields (`h->head`, `store->litlens`).

The sound spec is left in place (it verifies). The artifacts (`stubs/greedy_callees.c`, `_greedy_harness.c`, `_verify_greedy.sh`, `kill_greedy.sh`) are all intact. No memory update needed — the existing note is accurate. Per that documented investigation, this is a genuine "0 kills is inherent" case rather than a weak spec, so I'm not chasing the kills further.

lastPrompt: Verify ZopfliLZ77Greedy in /app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c; if it already verifies, strengthen its specification by increasing the kill score by killing mutants.
